topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, sharp and invasive against my skin. I stood near the entrance, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white, watching strangers mill about, their murmurs sounding like accusations. Then I saw it—my charcoal sketch, hanging prominently on the main wall, bathed in an unforgiving spotlight. It wasn't just displayed; it was framed, titled, presented as if it belonged to someone else entirely. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I took a shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor in my legs. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice thin and reedy, stepping forward. The curator, a man with an air of practiced indifference, merely glanced over, his eyes skimming past me as if I were a smudge on the pristine floor. The air seemed to thicken, suffocating me. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to bolt, to disappear into the shadows, but my feet remained rooted to the spot, paralyzed by a bewildering sense of violation.

[story 2]

I found myself lurking near the corner of the exhibition space, a shadow clinging to the periphery. Every time I dared to look toward the central display, a cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling my breath shallow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that echoed the frantic fluttering in my chest. Someone had taken my work—my raw, vulnerable pieces—and placed them under glass, turning my private expression into public property. I watched a group of patrons lean in, their whispered comments circling the artwork like vultures. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat; I clamped my mouth shut immediately, terrified the sound would betray the sheer panic churning inside me. I rubbed my arms quickly, a futile attempt to smooth down the sudden clamminess of my skin, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The silence in the room was oppressive, heavy enough to press down on my ears. I shuffled along the perimeter, my gaze repeatedly snagging on the canvas that was mine. It was positioned centrally, illuminated to draw the eye, and I could feel a sickening lurch in my gut. My jaw ached from clenching it so hard, and I kept running my fingers through my hair, a frantic, repetitive motion. When a woman paused nearby, her eyes flicked toward the painting, and I felt a surge of icy dread. I managed a strained, almost breathless, "That's mine," the words sounding hollow and weak even to my own ears. Her response was a dismissive shrug, a casual acknowledgment that made the blood drain from my face. I took a step back, my entire body vibrating with a sudden, overwhelming need to flee the suffocating atmosphere.
